title: Hash Map & Set
summary: Trade space for O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deduplication by mapping keys to indexed buckets.
overview: A hash map (object/Map in JS) stores key-value pairs with O(1) average lookup. A set stores unique values with O(1) membership tests. Both structures underlie the most common frontend optimization pattern: cache the result of a slow operation keyed by its input.
coreConcept: Hash functions convert arbitrary keys into array indices. Collisions are resolved via chaining or open addressing. In JavaScript, plain objects, Map, and Set all give you hash-table semantics. The core trade-off: use extra memory to eliminate repeated work. If you find yourself scanning an array repeatedly, a map is almost always the fix.
problemPatterns: Normalizing API responses by ID for O(1) access
Detecting duplicates or first non-repeating element
signals: You're doing repeated arr.find() or arr.includes() inside a loop
Question asks 'have you seen this before?'
You need to group items by a property
Lookup by ID from a flat array is a bottleneck

frontendScenarios.title: Normalizing API Response Data
frontendScenarios.description: An API returns an array of user objects. Components need to look up users by ID frequently. Converting the array to a Map<id, User> makes every lookup O(1) instead of O(n).

frontendScenarios.title: Deduplicating Tag Lists
frontendScenarios.description: A content editor lets users add tags. Tags must be unique and order-of-first-appearance matters. A Set handles both: insertion is O(1) and iteration preserves insertion order.

frontendScenarios.title: Redux-style Entities Slice
frontendScenarios.description: State management libraries normalize collections into { ids: string[], entities: Record<id, Item> }. This pattern lets the UI render lists (ids) while components read single items (entities[id]) in O(1).

dataThinking: Arrays are good at ordered access (index) and appending. Maps are good at keyed access. The moment you find yourself calling .find() inside a render loop or in a hot code path, normalize to a Map first. In React, this pattern often appears in useMemo: const itemMap = useMemo(() => new Map(items.map(i => [i.id, i])), [items]).

naiveApproach.description: Store data in an array; use .find() or .includes() for lookups. Simple to write, but O(n) per lookup.

betterApproach.description: Build a Map or Set upfront in O(n). Then every lookup is O(1) average. Total: O(n) time + O(n) space for the map.
betterApproach.complexity: O(n) build, O(1) lookup, O(n) space

visualizationSteps.label: Start: Two Sum problem
visualizationSteps.explanation: Looking for two numbers that sum to 9. Start with empty map. i=0, nums[0]=2.

visualizationSteps.label: i=0: Store 2 → 0
visualizationSteps.explanation: complement = 9 - 2 = 7. 7 not in map. Store seen[2]=0 and move on.

visualizationSteps.label: i=1: Complement found!
visualizationSteps.explanation: complement = 9 - 7 = 2. seen[2]=0 exists! Return [0, 1]. Only one pass through the array.

visualizationSteps.label: Result
visualizationSteps.explanation: O(n) time, O(n) space. Without the map, we'd need O(n²) with a nested loop.

practiceItems.title: First Non-Repeating Character
practiceItems.question: Given a string, find the first character that appears exactly once. Return its index, or -1. (This mirrors finding the first unique event type in an analytics stream.)
practiceItems.hint1: Two passes: first build a frequency map, then scan left to right for the first char with count 1.
practiceItems.hint2: Use a Map<string, number> to preserve insertion order and get accurate counts.

practiceItems.title: Normalize API Entities
practiceItems.question: You receive an array of Comment objects: { id: string, postId: string, text: string, authorId: string }[]. Write a function that groups them by postId, and separately indexes them by id. Return both structures.
practiceItems.hint1: One pass through the array can build both structures simultaneously.
practiceItems.hint2: Use Map<string, Comment[]> for the grouping and Map<string, Comment> for the index.

practiceItems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
practiceItems.question: Find the length of the longest substring with all unique characters. Classic sliding window + set problem that appears in token parsers and highlight range detection.
practiceItems.hint1: Use a Set to track characters in the current window. Expand right; when duplicate found, shrink from left.
practiceItems.hint2: A Map<char, index> lets you jump the left pointer directly to the right position instead of shrinking one by one.

reflectionPrompts: When should you use a plain object {} vs Map vs Set in JavaScript? What are the trade-offs?
Hash maps use O(n) extra space. When is that trade-off not worth it?
How does the 'normalize by ID' pattern in Redux/Zustand relate to database indexing?
In React, when would you put a Map inside useMemo vs computing it directly in render?
